text: Laparoscopic splenectomy anatomy is that laparoscopy generally the whenever you perform the massive splenectomy should be avoided and normally unless the spleen is double in the size it will be hidden into the rib cage so it has five ligament that is pancreas splenic ligament gastro splenic ligament phrenicoesplenic ligament, esplenocolic ligament, and phrenicocolic ligament. These all are the five ligaments which is important to keep in mind whenever you do laparoscopic esplenectomy.

Case selection is important. Normal indications are idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, microspherocytosis, benign tumors of the cyst, and HIV-related thrombocytopenia. There are certain contraindications also. Relative contraindication is hematological malignancies and moderate splenomegaly. And the absolute contraindication is massive splenomegaly or if the patient has the portal hypertension, In those situations, it should be avoided.

Patient position. Patient should be semi-lateral position. You will stand in front of the patient. One assistant will be behind the patient. Nurse should be on the right side next to the surgeon to give the instrument. And monitor, either you can one on the left side or you can take two also so that you and your assistant both can see. You have to put one cushion so that the costal margin will be little go up. And this is the usual position of the patient. That is semi-lateral position. The monitor will be behind and this will be the position of the patient.

The surgeon positions the laparoscopic ports approximately five to ten centimeters above the umbilicus, mostly on the midclavicular line, to avoid obstruction by the bowel and ensure visibility of the splenic hilum. A small incision is made, and a Veress needle is used to create pneumoperitoneum, followed by the insertion of a trocar. The telescope is introduced to visualize the spleen, adjusting the arc based on spleen size.

The surgeon demonstrates the baseball diamond concept, highlighting the target area at the splenic hilum, which visually resembles a baseball field or diamond shape. The port placement is compared to nephrectomy, with adjustments in position for the splenectomy procedure.

Because your toilet is shifting down. Yes. Vertically in the same line. And that also depends upon the size of kidney. Exactly same way as here it depends upon the size of the spleen. Now once you have entered, pneumo-peritoneum also has to be made in the same way. But it should not go vertical otherwise we can damage the bobble. So it should be perpendicular to the abdominal wall. And you will decide the position after putting the telescope. Because it may vary according to the size of the spleen.

The surgeon positions the ports for the splenectomy procedure, with the telescope port on the midclavicular line, and additional ports on the mid-axillary and posterior axillary lines. An optional epigastric port may be used for a liver retractor. The dissection begins with the inferior ligaments, including the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments, followed by the short gastric vessels, progressing towards the splenic hilum.

The surgeon uses bipolar, ligature, or harmonic devices to dissect the short gastric vessels. Endo-GI linear staplers may also be used. The surgical field then shifts to the hilum, where the vein is separated from the artery, prioritizing the artery for ligation. The kidney is retracted upwards to facilitate the separation of the splenocolic ligament.

The surgeon uses a hook, tripolar, or harmonic device to carefully dissect around the inferior pole of the spleen, ensuring the bowel is not injured. By freeing the inferior pole, the spleen and kidney are lifted, enhancing visibility of the short gastric vessels and the hilum. The splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments are detached, allowing the lower pole of the spleen to elevate.

The surgeon applies a titanium clip to control bleeding during the dissection of the short gastric vessels. A vascular stapler is used at the hilum, with a white cartridge fired over the vessels through a twelve mm port. The auto suture endo-GIA-II stapler is introduced, and upon firing, three rows of staples are applied on either side of the vessel, with slight oozing observed along the stapler line.

The surgeon uses the auto-suture Endo-GIA-II stapler to separate the splenic vessels, requiring two cartridges for complete separation. The stapler is introduced, spreading the tissue to avoid bunching, and the indicator turns green, confirming appropriate tissue thickness. The blade moves with each firing, cutting through the tissue, and the blade is retracted after firing. The frenico-asplenic ligament is detached using a hook.

The surgeon prepares to remove the spleen by enlarging the incision and using a commercially available D size endo bag with a self-retaining metal ring. The endo bag is inserted into the surgical site using a piston-like mechanism, and the spleen is maneuvered into the bag. A thread is pulled to apply a purse string closure around the spleen, securing it within the bag for extraction.

The surgeon removes the mouth of the endo bag from the abdominal cavity, and the assistant holds it while the spleen is mechanically morselated into small pieces using ovum forceps. The morselated tissue is placed into a kidney tray, ensuring no contamination or rupture of the endo bag to prevent splenosis. A nasogastric tube is inserted as a drain in the splenic fossa to manage any fluid collection. The wound is closed with stitches and cyanoacrylate glue, followed by the application of plaster adhesive. In cases of partial splenectomy, advanced stapler technology allows for preservation of spleen tissue, even in trauma cases.

The surgeon uses a stapler with a Gore-Tex sheath to complete the separation of the spleen, requiring one more cartridge for the final cut. The cut portion of the spleen is transferred into an endo bag for manual morselation.

The surgical field is irrigated and suctioned thoroughly, with minimal bleeding observed. A hematonic matrix is applied locally over the staple line to stop oozing, although it is not effective for massive bleeding.

These are the short gas sticks. You can coagulate at two places and then you can push the blade. It will cut in between.

The surgeon uses a tripolar instrument, which combines bipolar and cutting blade functions, to perform blunt and electrosurgical dissection of the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments. The harmonic device is used for dissection, while the ligature seals vessels up to seven mm. The short gastric vessels are carefully dissected, with attention to the tail of the pancreas near the hilum.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a placelet, following the French technique of lateral mobilization first, before addressing the inferior pole, short gastric vessels, and hilum of the spleen.

The surgeon mobilizes the spleen laterally without skeletonizing the artery, flipping the spleen over the stomach to access the hilum. Energy devices are used to facilitate this approach, which saves the cost of a stapler but requires careful and time-consuming manipulation.

The surgeon detaches remaining fibers of the splenocolic ligament after freeing the inferior pole, using a grasper to pull the bowel away and lift the spleen. A Maryland dissector is used to lift the spleen, ensuring its convexity is towards the spleen to prevent bleeding. Suction irrigation is employed to maintain a clear surgical field.

The spleen is now completely freed from the colon, revealing the tail of the pancreas. The surgeon approaches the hilum, which is lifted up, and begins careful dissection around the hilum to skeletonize the perihilar fat. The vein is visible above the artery, and the surgeon looks for an accessory spleen while creating a window between the vein and artery.

The surgeon lifts the inferior pole of the spleen and holds the artery with a Maryland dissector, creating a window between the artery and vein. Energy is used to seal the artery, avoiding the thin vein, and ensuring no bleeding occurs. The blade cuts the artery, and after a few minutes, the vein is collapsed and cut, securing the major vessels.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device to tackle the remaining short gastric vessels, visible alongside the stomach. The frenico-esplenic ligament is separated, and the lateral approach is demonstrated with the patient in a lateral position. The spleen is mobilized laterally using a hook to clear adhesions, and a peanut sponge is used for blunt dissection and pressure application to manage oozing. The spleen is flipped and mobilized, similar to open surgery techniques.

The spleen is completely mobilized and flipped, with its dorsal aspect touching the left lobe of the liver. The gastro-hepatic ligament is separated, and the hilum is visible with the artery and vein. Pulsations of the artery are observable. The perihilar fats are being separated, and a window is created between the artery and vein. A vascular stapler is used to secure the artery, followed by the vein. The spleen has slipped down, crossing the falciform ligament and almost touching the gallbladder. The stomach is visible below, and an endo bag is prepared for spleen removal.
